text: This is an instructional video demonstrating a right robotic XI nephroureterectomy with bladder cuff excision performed at Thomas Jefferson University Hospital in Our patient is a seventy four year old female who presented with a single episode of gross hematuria with clots. She has no history of stones or GU malignancy. She did have a prior twenty pack year smoking history and no additional toxin exposure. She had a negative urine culture, her serum creatinine was one point zero. Her CT urogram demonstrated a right kidney with a three point one times two point six times three point zero centimeter enhancing mass that filled the entire right collecting system with mild hydronephrosis. The patient subsequently underwent cystoscopy, ureteroscopy with biopsy confirming a high grade upper tract urothelial carcinoma without any evidence of disease in the bladder. Based on staging studies, she had clinical T-N-M-U-T-U-C.

She was counseled on the need for radical nephro-ureterectomy and the possible role for neoadjuvant chemotherapy. She opted to undergo dose-dense MVAC neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Following NAC, she was scheduled for robotic right radical NU regional lymphadenectomy and bladder cuff excision approximately six weeks later.

The patient is positioned in the left lateral decubitus position for the procedure. The XI robotic ports are placed, with a grasper inserted through a five millimeter port to retract the liver. The white line of Toldt is identified, allowing for the medial reflection of the ascending colon from the hepatic flexure to the cecum.

The surgeon identifies and mobilizes the duodenum, exposing the inferior vena cava (IVC) and renal vein. The dissection proceeds laterally to the IVC, revealing the psoas muscle beneath Girauda's fascia, with the gonadal vein and ureter visible over the psoas. The ureter is circumferentially undermined and clipped with two Hem-o-lok clips for oncologic control. The renal dissection continues with the kidney under tension, moving superiorly along the IVC to reach the renal hilum. The renal artery and vein are dissected free and ligated using a laparoscopic stapler.

The surgeon uses a ligature to mobilize the superior aspect of the right kidney while sparing the adrenal gland. The lateral and superior attachments remain intact to aid in traction during the ureteral dissection. Regional lymphadenectomy, including paracaval and intraaorticaval dissection, is performed.

The surgeon places the ureter on tension and dissects it along its length to the bladder, marked by the obliterated umbilical artery. The bladder is mobilized anteriorly for exposure, and entered superior to the ureteral orifice using electrocautery. The ureteral orifice is identified, and dissection is carried circumferentially to create a small bladder cuff. A V-lock absorbable suture is placed at the inferior apex for tension and closure aid. The bladder cuff is excised and closed with full thickness V-lock absorbable sutures bidirectionally, tied in the middle.

The surgeon fills the bladder with 120 to 180 cc of sterile water or saline to check for leaks at the bladder cuff. The peritoneal reflection is sutured closed, and the remaining lateral attachments of the kidney are dissected free.
